en.extract:
The Altai snowcock (Tetraogallus altaicus) is a species of bird in the family Phasianidae. It is found in western Mongolia and adjacent areas of China, Kazakhstan and Russia. Its natural habitat is boreal forests.


Taxonomy
The Altai snowcock was first described by Frederic Gebler in 1836. He was a doctor and naturalist who lived in the Altai region for forty years and named many previously unknown species. There are two subspecies, Tetraogallus altaicus altaicus and Tetraogallus altaicus orientalis.


Description
The Altai snowcock is a plump, partridge-like bird. While most snowcocks are similar in size, the Altai might be the largest species with a length of around 57 to 61 cm (22 to 24 in) and a mean body mass of approximately 2.54 kg (5.6 lb) in females and 3 kg (6.6 lb) in males. The head and neck are slatey-grey and there is a partial dark collar at the base of the neck. The upperparts are grey with white spots and the tail is black. The chin, breast and belly are white. The throat has a breastband of grey with patches of black. The primary and secondary feathers are black, the former having white bases which show as a white flash in flight. The beak is yellowish brown and the legs and feet reddish brown.


Distribution
The Altai snowcock is native to the mountain ranges of western Mongolia and southwestern Siberia. Its range includes in Russia the mountainous regions near Abakan, Sayan Mountains and the Tannu-Ola Mountains; and in Mongolia, the Mongolian Altai, the Govi-Altai, the Khangai Mountains, and the mountains east of Lake Khuvsgul. The habitat is open mountainside above the tree line at altitudes usually ranging between 2,000 and 3,600 metres (6,600 and 11,800 ft).


Behaviour

The Altai snowcock feeds on buds, shoots, roots, berries and insects and it also occasionally eats small rodents. About forty different species of plants have been recorded to be part of the diet.


Conservation
Populations of Altai snowcock are believed to be decreasing as a result of over-hunting and degradation of their habitat. However, in its Red List of Threatened Species, the IUCN lists this species as being of "Least Concern" because it has a wide distribution over an estimated 819,000 square kilometres (316,000 sq mi) and the number of mature birds is over 10,000 and not decreasing at such a rate as would justify their inclusion in a more vulnerable category.


References

The '''Altai snowcock''' ('''''Tetraogallus altaicus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Phasianidae]]. It is found in western [[Mongolia]] and adjacent areas of [[China]], [[Kazakhstan]] and [[Russia]]. Its natural [[habitat]] is boreal [[forest]]s.

==Taxonomy==
The Altai snowcock was first described by Frederic Gebler in 1836. He was a doctor and naturalist who lived in the Altai region for forty years and named many previously unknown species.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.museum.ru/museum/asrsm/exib/ad&a/chpt3en.htm |title=Frederic Gebler, Demidov Prize Laureate |work=A. N. Demidov. Historical - cultural heritage |publisher=Altai State Regional Studies Museum |access-date=2013-09-26}}</ref> There are two subspecies, ''Tetraogallus altaicus altaicus'' and ''Tetraogallus altaicus orientalis''.<ref name="avibase">{{cite web |url=http://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=D9A6E25C5C83C6F9 |title=Altai Snowcock (''Tetraogallus altaicus'') (Gebler, 1836)  |work=Avibase |publisher= |access-date=2013-09-26}}</ref>

==Description==
The Altai snowcock is a plump, partridge-like bird. While most snowcocks are similar in size, the Altai might be the largest species with a length of around {{convert|57|to|61|cm|in|abbr=on}} and a mean body mass of approximately {{convert|2.54|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in females and {{convert|3|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in males.<ref>McGowan, P. J. K. and G. M. Kirwan (2020). ''Altai Snowcock (Tetraogallus altaicus)'', version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. </ref><ref>MacKinnon, J. R., MacKinnon, J., Phillipps, K., & He, F. Q. (2000). ''A field guide to the birds of China''. Oxford University Press.</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses |edition=2nd |editor-first=John B. Jr. |editor-last=Dunning |publisher=CRC Press |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-4200-6444-5}}</ref> The head and neck are slatey-grey and there is a partial dark collar at the base of the neck. The upperparts are grey with white spots and the tail is black. The chin, breast and belly are white. The throat has a breastband of grey with patches of black. The primary and secondary feathers are black, the former having white bases which show as a white flash in flight. The beak is yellowish brown and the legs and feet reddish brown.<ref name=Madge>{{cite book |title=Pheasants, Partridges and Grouse: A Guide to the Pheasants, Partridges, Quails, Grouse, Guineafowl, Buttonquails and Sandgrouse of the World |last1=Madge |first1=Steve |last2=McGowan |first2=J. K. |last3=Kirwan |first3=Guy M. |year=2002 |publisher=A. C. Black |isbn=978-0-7136-3966-7 |pages=179–180 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_YLw38EoErYC&q=Tetraogallus+altaicus&pg=PP2 }}</ref>

==Distribution==
The Altai snowcock is native to the mountain ranges of western Mongolia and southwestern [[Siberia]]. Its range includes in Russia the mountainous regions near [[Abakan]], [[Sayan Mountains]] and the [[Tannu-Ola Mountains]]; and in Mongolia, the Mongolian [[Altai Mountains|Altai]], the [[Govi-Altai Province|Govi-Altai]], the [[Khangai Mountains]], and the mountains east of [[Khövsgöl Nuur|Lake Khuvsgul]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://birdsmongolia.blogspot.co.uk/2009/06/photospot-altai-snowcock-altai-snowcock.html |title=Photospot: Altai snowcock |author1=Bräunlich, Axel |author2=Buchheim, Andreas |work=Birding Mongolia |access-date=2013-09-26}}</ref> The habitat is open mountainside above the [[tree line]] at altitudes usually ranging between {{convert|2000|and|3600|m}}.<ref name=Madge/>

==Behaviour==
[[File:Tetraogallus altaicus - Andrew Bazdyrev - 335183633.jpeg|left|thumb|Foraging]]
The Altai snowcock feeds on buds, shoots, roots, berries and insects and it also occasionally eats small [[rodent]]s. About forty different species of plants have been recorded to be part of the diet.<ref name=Madge/>

==Conservation==
Populations of Altai snowcock are believed to be decreasing as a result of over-hunting and degradation of their habitat. However, in its [[Red List of Threatened Species]], the [[IUCN]] lists this species as being of "[[Least Concern]]" because it has a wide distribution over an estimated {{convert|819000|km2}} and the number of mature birds is over 10,000 and not decreasing at such a rate as would justify their inclusion in a more vulnerable category.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=135 |title=Species factsheet: ''Tetraogallus altaicus'' |publisher=BirdLife International |access-date=2013-09-26}}</ref>

zh.extract:
阿尔泰雪鸡（学名：Tetraogallus altaicus）是雉科雪鸡属的一种，主要分布于亚洲阿尔泰山脉地区的针叶林中。种加名 altaicus 意为“阿尔泰的”。


分类
阿尔泰雪鸡最早由奥地利博物学家弗里德里希·格布勒于1836年描述，格布勒在俄罗斯帝国的阿尔泰地区生活工作40余年，命名描述了多个物种。阿尔泰雪鸡有两个亚种：Tetraogallus altaicus altaicus和Tetraogallus altaicus orientalis。


形态特征
雄性成鸟头、颈为褐灰色，胸部有白色和黑色构成的圆斑。身体背部呈暗灰色，腹部白色。双翅上的羽毛边缘大部分部分为白色。体侧，腹部最中部和足羽黑褐色。
